title.scholar: The Triangular Trade and Middle Passage
title.explorer: A Terrible Journey Across the Ocean

summary.scholar: The Triangular Trade was a system of transatlantic commerce connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas. European goods were traded for enslaved Africans, who were transported across the Atlantic in the horrific Middle Passage — where an estimated 12.5 million Africans were forcibly shipped to the Americas between the 16th and 19th centuries. Approximately 2 million died during the voyage. In the Americas, enslaved people produced sugar, tobacco, and cotton exported to Europe.
summary.explorer: The Triangular Trade was a system where ships traveled between Europe, Africa, and America. The most terrible part was the Middle Passage — the journey across the Atlantic Ocean where enslaved African people were packed into ships under horrible conditions. About 12.5 million Africans were kidnapped and shipped to the Americas, and about 2 million died on the way. It was one of the greatest crimes in human history.

significance.scholar: The Triangular Trade and Middle Passage created the economic foundation of the colonial Atlantic world while inflicting incalculable suffering on millions of Africans and their descendants.
significance.explorer: The slave trade was one of the worst things in human history. Millions of people were stolen from their homes and forced into slavery.

sources.citation: Miller Center: U.S. Presidents and Key Events
